main_summary.summary: Rev. Dr. Samuel Berry McKinney, longtime pastor of Mount Zion Baptist Church in Seattle, was interviewed in Seattle on April 17 by David Cline of Virginia Tech for the Smithsonian, Library of Congress, and Southern Oral History Program. Born December 28, 1926, in Flint, Michigan, to a Baptist minister father (Wade Hampton McKinney, Morehouse 1920, Rochester Theological Seminary) and Spelman-educated mother Annie Ruth Berry McKinney, he grew up in Cleveland, Ohio, where his father served Antioch Baptist Church for over 34 years. McKinney entered Morehouse College in 1944 at age 17, in the same freshman class as 15-year-old Martin Luther King Jr., under President Benjamin Elijah Mays. He served in the segregated Army Air Corps during World War II, attended Colgate Rochester Divinity School, and was called to Mount Zion Baptist Church in Seattle in late 1957, beginning his ministry there in February 1958. McKinney recounts organizing King's 1961 Seattle visit and the First Presbyterian Church's last-minute cancellation, the Frederick & Nelson employment march, the Central Area Civil Rights Committee, founding work for Liberty Bank and the Seattle OIC under Rev. Leon H. Sullivan's national model, and the Samuel Berry McKinney Manor housing project.
key_themes: Morehouse College and Benjamin E. Mays as moral formation
Martin Luther King Jr. as Morehouse classmate
segregated military service in the Army Air Corps
Seattle ministry at Mount Zion Baptist Church
Pacific Northwest Black history and Oregon exclusion law
main_summary.historical_significance:
McKinney's testimony is a primary-source record of two convergent histories: the Atlanta University Center's early-20th-century Black educational network (Morehouse, Spelman, the Rockefeller and Rauschenbusch influences on the social gospel that shaped his father's generation and his own) and the Pacific Northwest civil rights movement, an under-documented regional theater of the broader struggle. His first-person account of being Martin Luther King Jr.'s Morehouse classmate establishes details rarely captured in standard King biographies, including the freshman-class context, King's age-15 admission via Georgia's wartime testing program, the 'Squirt' nickname, and King's record of never winning a Morehouse oratorical contest.

His account of the 1961 First Presbyterian Church incident in Seattle, the 1998 apology letter that followed, the Frederick & Nelson employment march, the Central Area Civil Rights Committee, the founding of Liberty Bank, and the establishment of the Seattle Opportunities Industrialization Center under Rev. Leon H. Sullivan's national framework documents how a Southern-trained Black Baptist pastor adapted Movement methods to the institutional and demographic conditions of the West Coast. McKinney's framing of the Oregon territory's Black exclusion law (repealed 1926), the George Washington Bush wagon-train story, and the regional migration patterns from west-of-Mississippi states give the interview ethnographic value as Pacific Northwest African American history.

chapters.title: Family Origins and Morehouse Lineage
chapters.summary: McKinney introduces his parents: father Wade Hampton McKinney, born outside Gainesville, Georgia, in 1892, who entered Atlanta Baptist College (later Morehouse) in 1910 and graduated in 1920, and mother Annie Ruth Berry McKinney of Birmingham, Alabama, who entered Spelman in 1916. He traces the founding of Morehouse (1867 in Augusta, named for Henry Lyman Morehouse in 1914) and Spelman (founded by Packard and Giles with Laura Spelman Rockefeller funding) within the post-Reconstruction Atlanta Baptist educational network.

chapters.title: Booker T. Washington and Black Land Ownership
chapters.summary: McKinney recounts his maternal grandfather's response to Booker T. Washington's 'let down your bucket where you are' message: acquiring land in Alabama and distributing it to his children. He links this to Dr. King's later Selma-to-Montgomery march stopping at Black-owned land along the route, and contrasts his maternal Baptist-minister grandfather with his paternal sharecropper grandfather.

chapters.title: Antioch Baptist and the Social Gospel
chapters.summary: His father took Antioch Baptist Church in Cleveland in 1928 and served there until his death in January 1963. McKinney describes the social-gospel tradition rooted in Luke 4 and Isaiah 61, traced through Rochester Theological Seminary and Walter Rauschenbusch, who died while his father was a student but whose mark remained on Morehouse-trained ministers.

chapters.title: Morehouse with Martin Luther King Jr.
chapters.summary: McKinney entered Morehouse in 1944 at 17 under President Benjamin Elijah Mays. His freshman class included 15-year-old Martin Luther King Jr., admitted via Georgia's wartime 11th-grade testing program. McKinney describes King as 'Squirt,' a 'city student' who drove to campus, who participated in every oratorical contest as a student but never won one, and who went on to Crozer Theological Seminary and Boston University.

chapters.title: Cleveland Childhood and Civil Rights Influences
chapters.summary: McKinney recalls growing up in Cleveland from age 18 months, watching his father confront injustice and hosting figures including A. Philip Randolph, Walter White of the NAACP, Mordecai Wyatt Johnson, and Adam Clayton Powell Jr. He describes the Future Outlook League's 'don't patronize where you cannot work' boycotts, including the Cleveland Transit System and milk-delivery campaigns.

chapters.title: Segregated Army Air Corps Service
chapters.summary: After basic training at Sheppard Field in Wichita Falls, Texas, McKinney was shipped to Yuma, Arizona. On the rail journey through West Texas he witnessed German prisoners of war eating in the white dining room with tablecloths and silverware while Black U.S. soldiers in uniform ate from cardboard plates in the colored dining room. He was assigned to Squadron F, the segregated Black unit. The G.I. Bill (one year for any service member active September 2, 1945, plus one month per month of service up to 48 months) funded his and his brother's college completion.

chapters.title: Call to Ministry and Joe Louis Era
chapters.summary: McKinney shifted from pre-law to ministry under the influence of Morehouse Bible instructor George Kelsey, who used the metaphor of the law functioning at the back end while religion functions at the front end. He recalls Cleveland's collective listening to Joe Louis fights, particularly the Schmeling defeat that the Jewish community celebrated as a defeat of one of Hitler's boys, and the presence of Jesse Owens's family in the Cleveland neighborhood. Larry Doby's integration of the American League Cleveland Indians similarly mobilized the community.
related_events: Joe Louis vs Max Schmeling fight

chapters.title: Inviting Dr. King to Seattle in 1961
chapters.summary: McKinney invited Martin Luther King Jr. to speak in Seattle in 1961 for the Mount Zion Baptist Church Brotherhood. With the 1962 World's Fair construction making facilities scarce, the Presbytery of Seattle directed him to First Presbyterian Church downtown. After an antecedent agreement, the ruling body of First Presbyterian withdrew the venue in writing, citing protocol. McKinney refused their offered reimbursement, the event moved to an alternative downtown venue, and 37 to 38 years later, just before his 1998 retirement, First Presbyterian sent a formal letter of apology.

chapters.title: Reparations and James Forman's Black Manifesto
chapters.summary: Asked about James Forman's 1969 Black Manifesto's call for reparations, McKinney connects reparations to apology as a necessary part of healing. He references the Japanese American internment as a domestic precedent for apology plus financial redress, noting Japanese farmlands taken near Boeing Field and Puyallup after Pearl Harbor.

chapters.title: Pacific Northwest Black History
chapters.summary: McKinney narrates the Oregon Black exclusion law (not repealed until 1926), the Louisiana Purchase and the dispute over slavery in new territory, and the George Washington Bush story: a Black wagon-train leader from Missouri who, denied permanent residence in the Oregon Territory, crossed the Columbia River to Fort Vancouver and settled near Lacey/Olympia. He links Bush's son's service in the last territorial and first state legislatures of Washington to the 49th-parallel resolution of the U.S.-British Columbia boundary and the Pig War on the San Juan Islands. He also traces coal mining at Roslyn, where Black scab labor was brought to break unions, and Kennydale, with a coal vein running under Puget Sound.

chapters.title: Arriving in Seattle and the Juneteenth Factor
chapters.summary: McKinney accepted the call to Mount Zion in late November 1957 and began the first Sunday in February 1958. He draws a migration map by region for Black churches he served: Providence Rhode Island drew from Virginia in World War I and North Carolina in World War II; Cleveland from Georgia, Alabama, Kentucky, and Tennessee; Detroit from many places via the car industry; Chicago from Mississippi; and Seattle from west of the Mississippi (Louisiana, Texas, Arkansas, Oklahoma). He introduces what he calls the 'Juneteenth factor' learned in Texas during basic training: Texas slaves did not learn of emancipation until June 19, 1865, nearly two and a half years after the Emancipation Proclamation took effect.

chapters.title: Frederick & Nelson and Central Area Civil Rights Committee
chapters.summary: McKinney describes the Seattle march on Frederick & Nelson department store that opened Black employment, with Rev. John Adams of First AME serving as a leader of the Central Area Civil Rights Committee (CACRC), an umbrella body drawing NAACP, Urban League, and (later) CORE. He cites the school strike as one of its actions and notes that Black Panther organizing in 1968-69 created tension with the established ministry leadership.

chapters.title: Liberty Bank, OIC, and the McKinney Manor
chapters.summary: McKinney describes parallel work of protest and building: Mount Zion's credit union, land acquisition for parking and church expansion, the construction of the sanctuary (with American Baptist Home Mission Society support after local banks refused), the Samuel Berry McKinney Manor housing project funded under government programs, the Seattle Opportunities Industrialization Center at 22nd and Jackson based on Rev. Leon H. Sullivan's Philadelphia model, the Central Area Motivation Program, and his role in organizing Liberty Bank.

chapters.title: Reflections on Obama, Coalition, and the Future
chapters.summary: McKinney closes by reflecting on changes in the Central Area's demographics with African immigrant communities arriving, the inadequacy of treating Hispanic as a single category, and his political analysis of the Obama presidency, the resistance he faced, and the necessity of multiracial coalitions of Black, Latino, and Asian communities against efforts to divide them.
